items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: Correct answer: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Dots. “Lines” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
